name: Query performance degraded after 6 months in production
description: A Postgres-backed app was fast at launch but queries are now taking 2-8 seconds. The products table has 2M rows, orders has 12M, order_items has 45M. The slowest query joins all three to show 'top 10 products by revenue in the last 30 days'.
task:
Diagnose and fix this slow Postgres query:

```sql
SELECT p.id, p.name, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON oi.product_id = p.id
JOIN orders o ON o.id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
  AND o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.id, p.name
ORDER BY revenue DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

Row counts: products 2M, orders 12M, order_items 45M. Currently takes 2-8s. Provide: (1) your analysis of what's wrong, (2) the EXPLAIN ANALYZE output you'd expect, (3) specific index additions (with CREATE INDEX statements), (4) any query rewrite, (5) additional schema or precomputation if needed. Target: <100ms.
expected_outcomes: Identifies missing compound index on orders(status, created_at)
Suggests index on order_items(order_id, product_id) with covered columns
Considers materialized view or precomputed aggregation
Discusses date range partitioning for orders table
Target improvement is realistic with reasoning
